coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot aggregates: A new strategy to promote corrosion inhibition performance of carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Yi
He, Haijie
Qiao, Hongxia
Wang, Shifang
He, Chuang
Xiang, Tengfei
coredata.dc:description: The corrosion of metal not only produces serious economic losses and environmental pollution but also threatens equipment safety. Carbon dots (CDs) exhibit outstanding inhibition properties to availably retard the corrosion of metal. However, the present CDs-based corrosion inhibitors still fail to fully exert the environmentally friendly and low-cost merits of CDs. To address this issue, CD aggregates (CDAs) prepared by low-cost and eco-friendly CDs are developed as novel corrosion inhibitors for the first time, and their corrosion inhibition mechanism is disclosed. Specifically, CDAs with excellent long-term dispersion stability are synthesized by solvothermal treatment of CDs formed by citric acid and urea. CDAs at only 200 mg/L provide a corrosion inhibition effect of 90.38% for Q235 carbon steel in 1 mol/L HCl solution, approximately 1.53 times higher than that of CDs (59.21%). Moreover, the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDAs is proposed through the analysis of adsorption isotherm, examination of corrosion morphologies, and theoretical calculations. That is, CDAs physically and chemically adsorb on the carbon steel surface, mainly hindering its anodic reaction, thus retarding the corrosion of carbon steel. This research firstly verifies the corrosion inhibition potential of green and low-cost CDAs and provides a novel strategy to promote the corrosion inhibition performance of CDs, promoting the progress of aggregate-based corrosion inhibitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregates
Carbon dots
Corrosion inhibition mechanism
Adsorption
Carbon steel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund (No. 52208273), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LQ21E080003), and \u201CPioneer\u201D and \u201CLeading Goose\u201D R&D Program of Zhejiang (No. 2022C03051; 2023C04033). Furthermore, the authors thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for XPS measurement.

This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund (No. 52208273), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LQ21E080003), and \u201CPioneer\u201D and \u201CLeading Goose\u201D R&D Program of Zhejiang, China (No. 2022C03051; 2023C04033). Furthermore, the authors thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for XPS measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart of CDAs synthesis.
TEM: (a) CDs and (b) CDAs; (c) Raman spectra.
(a) FTIR spectra; Full-scan XPS: (b) CDs and (c) CDAs; High-resolution C1s, O1s, and N1s spectra: (d–f) CDs and (g–i) CDAs.
UV–vis spectra (insets are optical photos taken in daylight and at 365 nm UV light) and PL spectra in aqueous solution: (a, b) CDs and (c, d) CDAs.
Pictures of (a) CDs and (b) CDAs with diverse contents in 1 mol/L HCl solution in daylight and 365 nm UV light after standing for two weeks.
UV–vis and PL spectra of (a, b) CDs and (c, d) CDAs with unlike additions in 1 mol/L HCl solution.
UV–vis and PL spectra of (a, b) CDs and (c, d) CDAs with disparate contents in 1 mol/L HCl solution before and after standing for two weeks.
The v corr and η values in different states: (a, b) CDs and (c, d) CDAs.
Nyquist, Bode, and PDP in various situations: (a–c) CDs and (d–f) CDAs.
An equivalent circuit model adopted to fit the EIS results.
Adsorption isotherm fitted from EIS and PDP data: (a, b) CDs and (c, d) CDAs.
3D images and height profiles of carbon steel samples under diverse conditions: (a, d) blank group; (b, e) with 200 mg/L CDs; (c, f) with 200 mg/L CDAs.
SEM and EDS of carbon steel samples in various situations: (a, d) blank group; (b, e) with 200 mg/L CDs; (c, f) with 200 mg/L CDAs.
High-resolution O1s, Fe2p, and N1s spectra of the specimens under various states: (a, d) blank group; (b, e, g) with 200 mg/L CDs; (c, f, h) with 200 mg/L CDAs.
Simplified molecular configuration, HOMO, and LUMO of (a–c) CDs and (d–f) CDAs.
The side surface and the top surface of the equilibrium adsorption configuration: (a, b) CDs and (c, d) CDAs.
Corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDAs for carbon steel in 1 mol/L HCl solution.
The elementary component of Q235 carbon steel (by mass percent).
The v corr and η values of CDs and CDAs in diverse states.
The parameters obtained from fitting EIS data in different cases.
The parameters gained from PDP data under diverse conditions.
Element content of carbon steel surfaces in diverse states.
Element compositions from XPS and fitted ratio from high-resolution O1s and Fe2p spectra.
The quantum chemical parameters of CDs and CDAs and their binding energy on the Fe (110) surface.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot aggregates: A new strategy to promote corrosion inhibition performance of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Anhui University of Technology, Ma'anshan, Anhui, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Anhui University of Technology, Ma'anshan, Anhui, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The corrosion of metal not only produces serious economic losses and environmental pollution but also threatens equipment safety. Carbon dots (CDs) exhibit outstanding inhibition properties to availably retard the corrosion of metal. However, the present CDs-based corrosion inhibitors still fail to fully exert the environmentally friendly and low-cost merits of CDs. To address this issue, CD aggregates (CDAs) prepared by low-cost and eco-friendly CDs are developed as novel corrosion inhibitors for the first time, and their corrosion inhibition mechanism is disclosed. Specifically, CDAs with excellent long-term dispersion stability are synthesized by solvothermal treatment of CDs formed by citric acid and urea. CDAs at only 200 mg/L provide a corrosion inhibition effect of 90.38% for Q235 carbon steel in 1 mol/L HCl solution, approximately 1.53 times higher than that of CDs (59.21%). Moreover, the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDAs is proposed through the analysis of adsorption isotherm, examination of corrosion morphologies, and theoretical calculations. That is, CDAs physically and chemically adsorb on the carbon steel surface, mainly hindering its anodic reaction, thus retarding the corrosion of carbon steel. This research firstly verifies the corrosion inhibition potential of green and low-cost CDAs and provides a novel strategy to promote the corrosion inhibition performance of CDs, promoting the progress of aggregate-based corrosion inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:para: On account of its high strength, strong plasticity, and excellent electrical/thermal conductivity, metal is widely utilized in construction, industrial manufacturing, electronic products, and other fields [1–3]. Nevertheless, metal is prone to electrochemical corrosion. This not only results in severe economic losses but also endangers production safety and pollutes the environment [4]. According to the National Association of Corrosion Engineers, the annual global cost of corrosion exceeds $2.5 trillion, accounting for approximately 4.2% of gross domestic product [5]. Pickling is commonly performed on metal to remove its corrosion products [6]. However, the pickling solution inevitably damages the metal matrix. Hence, the corrosion inhibitor is introduced into acidic media to restrain the corrosion of metal.

Traditional corrosion inhibitors such as chromates, mercury salts, and nitrites are effective in inhibiting corrosion [7]. Nevertheless, they contain toxic elements, polluting the environment. Green corrosion inhibitors such as amino acid [8] and ionic liquid [9] have been developed to meet environmental requirements, but still suffer from high costs. Exhilaratingly, carbon dots (CDs) as an innovative class of carbon-based nanomaterials, possess the advantages of cost-effectiveness, biocompatibility, low toxicity, simple preparation, and a wide source of raw materials [10,11]. More importantly, CDs exhibit outstanding corrosion inhibition properties [12–14]. For example, Zhao's group prepared CDs using 4-aminosalicylic acid as the precursor and disclosed that the corrosion inhibition effect of 100 mg/L CDs for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution was 87.2% [15]. Subsequently, Wang's group synthesized p-CDs and o-CDs adopting p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, respectively. Their corrosion inhibition efficiencies at 200 mg/L for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution were 88% and 94%, respectively [16]. Although the aforementioned CDs realize splendid corrosion inhibition performance, the adopted precursors are toxic and expensive, failing to exert the environmentally friendly and low-cost advantages of CDs. This is not conducive to their popularization and application.

It is well known that CDs prepared from citric acid and urea are inexpensive and non-toxic. Nevertheless, the corrosion inhibition effect of such CDs is unsatisfactory in our previous experiments. If the corrosion inhibition ability of such CDs is improved, it will contribute to developing green and low-cost CDs-based corrosion inhibitors. Notably, aggregation is probably a useful approach to enhance the corrosion inhibition capacity of nanomaterials [17]. For instance, Zhang's group synthesized 4-((1H-indol-3-yl)methyl)phenol (IMP) and 4-(di(1Hindol-3-yl)methyl)phenol (DIMP) nano-aggregates based on aggregate preparation, and disclosed that their corrosion inhibition efficiencies at 2 mM for copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution were 99.3% and 97.5%, respectively [17]. Up to now, researchers have studied the optical performance of CD aggregates (CDAs) employing citric acid and urea as raw materials [18]. However, to our knowledge, there is no literature on the corrosion inhibition property of CDAs, not to mention their corrosion inhibition mechanism.

To prove the feasibility of the aggregation strategy and fully exert the environmentally friendly and low-cost advantages of CDs, this work firstly adopts CDAs as a low-cost and green corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel. And the corrosion inhibition performance and corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDAs are comprehensively studied. To be specific, CDAs are prepared by solvothermal treatment of CDs produced from urea and citric acid. Subsequently, their corrosion inhibition abilities at different concentrations for carbon steel in 1 mol/L HCl solution are evaluated by the weight loss test and electrochemical measurements. The corrosion inhibition effect of CDAs (90.38%) is greatly promoted compared to that of CDs (59.21%). Furthermore, the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDAs is comprehensively revealed by combining the analysis of adsorption isotherm, examination of corrosion morphologies, and theoretical calculations. This work verifies the corrosion inhibition potential of CDAs for the first time, providing a new idea for the development and utilization of CDs-based corrosion inhibitors and offering a reference for the research of other aggregate-related corrosion inhibitors.

The elementary component of Q235 carbon steel samples is exhibited in Table 1 . The samples were burnished with sandpaper grades from 600 to 2000 and cleaned with deionized (DI) water and alcohol. Citric acid, urea, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol, and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl) were gained from Shanghai Merck Chemical Co., Ltd.

CDs were synthesized by microwave method. Concretely, citric acid (3 g) and urea (6 g) were mixed into DI water (20 mL) and then heated for 5 min in a household microwave (750 W). Subsequently, the solution changed from colorless to brown, and eventually to a dark brown clumpy solid, manifesting the generation of CDs. And the aqueous solution of CDs was undergone centrifugation (8000 rpm, 20 min) to eliminate large or agglomerated particles. Finally, the solution was dialyzed using a dialysis bag with MWCO of 0.1–0.5 KDa to obtain a clear yellow-brown solution of CDs.

Inspired by the literature [18], CDAs were produced by the solvothermal method of CDs in DMF. To be specific, 70 mg/mL CDs DMF solution was heated for 2 h at 220 °C. Subsequently, the resulting dark-colored solution was blended with double the volume of ethanol and passed centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) to eliminate the remaining byproducts. Ultimately, the resulting sediment went through freeze-drying to form dark-colored powders of CDAs.

The morphologies of CDs and CDAs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S, USA). The structural information of CDs and CDAs was surveyed by adopting Raman spectra (Thermo Scientific DXR, USA) with an optical maser wavelength of 1064 nm and a wavelength range of 1000–1800 cm−1. The surface groups and elemental components of CDs and CDAs were examined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet-6700, USA) within the range of 1000–4000 cm−1 and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250, USA). Furthermore, the optical characteristics of CDs and CDAs were recorded employing ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis, PerkinElmer Lambda 750, USA) and photoluminescence (PL, Hitachi F7000, Japan) spectra.

The carbon steel samples measuring 15 mm × 15 mm × 3 mm were exposed to 1 mol/L HCl solution with diverse contents of CDs/CDAs. And they were removed at 24, 48, 72, and 96 h, respectively. Subsequently, they were rinsed with DI water and alcohol, dried, and weighed three times using an electronic scale. The corrosion rate (v corr) and inhibition efficiency (η) were determined by
v corr
=
Δ W
A t
and
η =
v corr
‐
v corr ′
v corr
× 100 %
, respectively, where ΔW expressed the mean weight loss of carbon steel (g); A indicated the carbon steel surface area (cm2); t represented the experiment period (h); v corr and v'corr presented the corrosion rates of carbon steel (mg∙cm−2∙h−1) in 1 mol/L HCl solution without and with CDs/CDAs respectively.

The corrosion inhibition ability of CDs/CDAs for carbon steel samples in 1 mol/L HCl solution was examined by a PARSTAT 4000+ electrochemical workstation. Specifically, a conventional three-electrode setup was used, featuring the platinum plate as the counter electrode (CE), the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode (RE), and the sample as the working electrode (WE) with an exposed surface area of 1 cm2. Prior to experiments, the electrodes were exposed to 1 mol/L HCl solution including various contents of CDs/CDAs for 1 h at room temperature. And it was essential to wait for half an hour for the open circuit potential (OCP) to stabilize before conducting electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) curve measurements. EIS was scanned at an amplitude of 10 mV in a frequency range from 105 to 10−2 Hz, and PDP was scanned at a scan rate of 1 mV/s within a frequency range of ±200 mV. ZSimDemo 3.3 and CorrView 3.0 software were adopted to fit and analyze the EIS and PDP data, respectively.

After 96-h exposure to 1 mol/L HCl solution with diverse additions of CDs/CDAs, the carbon steel specimens were removed, rinsed with DI water and alcohol, and dried. The corrosion morphologies of the samples were examined by 3D scanner (Keyence VR-6000, Japan) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Sigma 300, Germany) fitted with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer (EDS). And the element information on the specimen surface was revealed by XPS.

Molecular simulation calculation was conducted by Material Studio 7.0 software. Specifically, the electron-gaining and electron-losing capacities of corrosion inhibitor molecules were assessed by calculating quantum chemical parameters employing density functional theory (DFT). Quantum chemical calculations were performed using the GGA/BLYP method with a double numerical basis set including d and p polarization. The convergence quality was set to fine, the energy tolerance value was 10−5 Ha, and the maximum displacement was 0.005 Å. Molecular dynamic (MD) simulations were adopted to reveal the adsorption behavior of corrosion inhibitor molecules on the Fe surface. Fe (110) surface was employed as a model to represent the carbon steel surface. Furthermore, the interactions between the corrosion inhibitor molecules and the Fe (110) surface were modeled within a simulation box with dimensions of 36 Å∗29 Å∗44 Å, containing 6 layers of Fe (110) surface, 300 water molecules, 15 H+, 15 Cl-, and 1 CDs/CDAs molecule. MD simulations were conducted utilizing the COMPASS II force field in a 298 K NVT system with a total duration of 500 ps and an interval of 1 fs.

CDAs are produced by solvothermal treatment of CDs (Fig. 1 ). The morphologies of CDs and CDAs are determined by TEM (Fig. 2 a and b). Both CDAs and CDs are spherical with uniform distribution. Noticeably, the mean size of CDAs (ca. 25.62 nm) is significantly larger than that of CDs (ca. 2.13 nm). The structural information of CDs and CDAs is characterized by Raman spectra (Fig. 2c). They both display two obvious peaks at 1352 and 1576 cm−1 corresponding to the D peak (sp3 hybrid C) and G peak (sp2 C), respectively [19]. A lower intensity ratio of D and G peaks (I D/I G) corresponds to a higher crystallinity. Obviously, the lower I D/I G ratio of CDAs (0.89) than that of CDs (0.98) verifies that CDAs possess a higher crystallinity. To sum up, CDAs are possibly formed by further carbonization and dehydration condensation of CDs.

The surface groups and elemental components of CDs and CDAs are disclosed by FTIR spectra and XPS. CDAs and CDs have similar surface groups (Fig. 3 a). The peaks at 3465–3441, 3338–3204, 2925–2803, 1714–1710, 1622–1612, 1460–1402, and 1190–1059 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H, N–H, C–H, CO, CC, C–N, and C–O–C, respectively [12,20,21]. The affluent N- and O-containing surface groups of CDs and CDAs endow them with excellent water solubility. In striking contrast to CDs, CDAs own a decreased content of CO and an increased content of CC, indicating a possible dehydration condensation reaction during the synthesis of CDAs as well as the extension of the sp2 conjugated domain [18]. This is in agreement with Raman spectra. With respect to full-scan XPS, three distinct peaks (Fig. 3b and c) at 285, 400, and 531 eV correspond to C, N, and O elements, respectively. Notably, the C content of CDAs (61.35%) is larger than that of CDs (49.58%), further confirming that CDs undergo carbonization and dehydration condensation to form CDAs. High-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 3d and g) display three peaks belonging to C–C/CC (284.8 ± 0.3 eV), CO (288.4 ± 0.3 eV), and CN (289.5 ± 0.3 eV) [12,14]. Particularly, the C–C/CC content of CDAs (50.21%) is greater than that of CDs (42.88%), indicating that CDAs possess a higher crystallinity, consistent with FTIR spectra. High-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 3e and h) exhibit three peaks attributed to CO (531.0 ± 0.1 eV), C–O/C–OH (531.7 eV), and OC–O (533.6 ± 0.4 eV) [18]. High-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. 3f and i) demonstrate two peaks assigned to C–N (399.5 ± 0.2 eV) and –NH2 (400.5 ± 0.8 eV) [22]. Significantly, C–O/C–OH and –NH2 contents of CDAs are lower than those of CDs, suggesting that the dehydration condensation reaction occurs between the hydroxyl and amino groups of CDs during solvothermal treatment. The aforementioned characterization consequences manifest that CDAs are aggregated from CDs by further carbonization and dehydration condensation.

The optical characteristics of CDs and CDAs are recorded using UV–vis and PL spectra. Notably, the absorption peak redshifts from 410 nm (CDs) to 418 nm (CDAs) (Fig. 4 a and c), suggesting that the increased carbonization of the carbon core after solvothermal treatment leads to the extension of π-π∗ conjugation [18]. The aqueous solution of CDs/CDAs is yellow/pale yellow under daylight. And under 365 nm UV light, CDs/CDAs emit green/blue fluorescence. The PL spectra (Fig. 4b and d) indicate that CDs display an excitation-independent PL behavior, while CDAs show excitation-dependent PL behavior. Furthermore, the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of CDs and CDAs are both 460/529 nm. The aforementioned results provide an innovative approach to appraise the dispersion stability of CDAs.

In summary, well-dispersed CDAs are successfully prepared from CDs by further carbonization and dehydrated condensation. CDAs possess a higher crystallinity compared with CDs. In addition, a multitude of N- and O-containing surface groups of CDAs endow them with wonderful water solubility, providing a strong basis for their utilization as corrosion inhibitors in acidic solutions.

The weight loss test is a dependable way to evaluate corrosion inhibition performance [26]. Fig. 8 and Table 2 display the v corr values of carbon steel and η values of CDs and CDAs under different states. The v corr value of the blank group improves with increasing immersion time. After adding CDs/CDAs, the v corr values decrease with prolonged immersion time. Under identical immersion time, the v corr values reduce with increasing concentrations of CDs/CDAs within the range of 200 mg/L. Notably, the corrosion inhibition effect of CDs is unsatisfactory regardless of immersion time or concentration. In sharp contrast, the corrosion inhibition capacity of CDAs is greatly enhanced, indicating that CDAs provide better protection for carbon steel. Specifically, after 96-h exposure to 1 mol/L HCl solution, 200 mg/L CDAs decline the v corr value by 83.02%, about 1.47 times larger than that of CDs (56.34%). Correspondingly, CDAs own higher η values than CDs irrespective of immersion time and concentration. Concretely, the η value of 200 mg/L CDAs reaches 81.70%, approximately 1.55 times bigger than that of CDs (52.78%). The aforementioned consequences suggest that CDAs offer greater long-term corrosion protection for carbon steel than CDs.

The corrosion inhibition property of CDAs is further investigated by EIS and PDP. EIS is adopted to analyze the interface performance of carbon steel and solution. The capacitance arcs are semicircular regardless of the incorporation of CDs/CDAs (Fig. 9 a and d), revealing that CDs/CDAs fail to change the electrochemical properties of carbon steel electrodes during the corrosion process [27]. After adding CDs/CDAs, the capacitance arc diameters are larger than the blank group, and there is a positive relationship between capacitance arc diameter and the content of CDs/CDAs. More importantly, the capacitive arc diameters of the CDAs-containing groups are much bigger than those of the CDs-containing groups under an identical concentration, suggesting that CDAs inhibit charge transfer more effectively [28]. This may be attributed to the fact that the protective film formed by the adsorption of CDAs on the carbon steel surface is more effective and denser, thus providing a stronger inhibition effect [29]. In general, the higher the impedance modulus at 0.01 Hz (|Z|0.01 Hz), the greater the corrosion inhibition property [30,31]. The addition of CDs/CDAs improves the |Z|0.01 Hz values compared with the blank group, and the|Z|0.01 Hz values increase with more content of CDs/CDAs in the range of 200 mg/L. This indicates that CDs/CDAs can protect carbon steel from corrosion, the higher the concentration, the better the corrosion inhibition effect. Noticeable, compared with the CDs-containing groups (Fig. 9b), the |Z|0.01 Hz values of the CDAs-containing groups are greater (Fig. 9e). Specifically, the |Z|0.01 Hz value achieves 195 Ω cm2 at 200 mg/L CDAs, around 2.27 times higher than that of CDs (86 Ω cm2), verifying that CDAs inhibit carbon steel corrosion better than CDs. Moreover, the Bode-phase peak values of the CDAs-containing groups are larger than those of the CDs-containing groups at the same content, manifesting that the capacitive response caused by CDAs adsorption on the steel surface is stronger [32].

An equivalent circuit (Fig. 10 ) is utilized to fit the data to better understand the EIS results. The parameters (Table 3 ) gained from the fitting are solution resistance (R s), polarization resistance (R p), and double-layer capacitance (CPEdl). The R s values change little after adding CDs/CDAs, manifesting CDs/CDAs have a small effect on the R s values. The R p values are employed to confirm the inhibition efficiency (γ): γ = (R p-R p0)/R p × 100%, where R p and R p0 present the polarization resistance with and without the introduction of CDs/CDAs, respectively [14]. By comparison with the blank group, the presence of CDs/CDAs improves the γ values, and the γ values boost with the augment of CDs/CDAs addition in the range of 200 mg/L. Significantly, the γ values of the CDAs-containing groups are larger than those of the CDs-containing groups under the same content. Concretely, the γ value of 200 mg/L CDAs is 83.30%, roughly 1.41 times higher than that of CDs (59.21%). This proves that CDAs possess better corrosion inhibition ability than CDs, consistent with the weight loss test. Generally, the CPEdl value is related to the corrosion area, determined by the formula: CPEdl = Y 0(2πf max)n−1 [33], where Y 0 is the modulus of CPEdl [34], f max denotes frequency at the maximum value of impedance imaginary component, and n indicates the homogeneity of the steel surface. Compared with the blank group, the CPEdl values descend after incorporating CDs/CDAs. And the CPEdl values decrease with increasing concentration of CDs/CDAs within the range of 200 mg/L. Noticeably, CDAs lead to a greater reduction in CPEdl values compared with CDs. This testifies that CDAs possess a stronger adsorption capacity on the carbon steel surface, thus more availably reducing the area subjected to corrosion. Furthermore, as the n value approaches 1, the carbon steel surface becomes increasingly homogeneous [13]. Notably, the incorporation of CDs/CDAs improves the n values compared to the blank group and the n values are proportional to the contents of CDs/CDAs. Obviously, the n values of the CDAs-containing groups are greater than those of the CDs-containing groups at an equal content. This is ascribed to the greater corrosion inhibition performance of CDAs, resulting in a more homogeneous carbon steel surface. The above EIS consequences confirm that CDAs own a stronger adsorption ability on the carbon steel surface than CDs, contributing to a greater protection effect against corrosion.

PDP is employed to evaluate the anodic and cathodic reactions of carbon steel and the inhibition effect of CDAs on these reactions. The anodic and cathodic branches of the polarization curves shift toward lower currents with the incorporation of CDs/CDAs compared with the blank group (Fig. 9c and f), implying that CDs and CDAs restrain both anodic and cathodic reactions of carbon steel [35]. The parameters (Table 4 ) acquired from PDP data are self-corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), cathodic slope (β c), anodic slope (β a), and inhibition efficiency (IE). By comparison with the blank group, the E corr value moves toward the anodic direction within the range of 85 mV (−504 ∼ −483 mV) after adding CDs/CDAs, meaning that CDs and CDAs are mixed-type corrosion inhibitors [7]. Moreover, in contrast to the blank group, the β c and β a values are smaller in the presence of CDs/CDAs, and these values descend with more content of CDs/CDAs. Notably, the β c and β a values of the CDAs-containing groups decline faster than those of the CDs-containing groups (especially the β a values), signifying that CDAs restrain the anodic and cathodic reactions of carbon steel more evidently (mainly inhibit the anodic reaction). The i corr values are utilized to calculate the IE value: IE = (i0 corr-i corr)/i0 corr × 100%, where i0 corr and i corr signify the corrosion current density without and with the incorporation of CDs/CDAs, respectively [12]. After adding CDs/CDAs, the IE values become higher compared with the blank group. There is a positive correlation between the IE values and the concentration of CDs/CDAs in the range of 200 mg/L. Obviously, the IE values of the CDAs-containing groups are larger than those of the CDs-containing groups. Specifically, the IE value of 200 mg/L CDAs is 90.38%, approximately 1.77 times higher than that of CDs (51.07%), confirming the better corrosion inhibition capacity of CDAs, in alignment with the EIS results. The aforementioned PDP consequences express that CDAs inhibit the anodic reaction of carbon steel more effectively than CDs, thus greater retarding carbon steel corrosion.

In conclusion, CDAs exhibit a stronger adsorption capacity on the carbon steel surface compared with CDs and restrain the anodic reaction of carbon steel more effectively, thus providing a better long-term corrosion inhibition effect. To be more specific, the inhibition efficiency of 200 mg/L CDAs is 90.38% in 1 mol/L HCl solution, about 1.53 times larger than that of CDs (59.21%).

The adsorption behavior of corrosion inhibitors on the metal surface is researched by adopting the adsorption isotherm. According to the formula: C inh/θ = 1/K ads + C inh, where θ represents the surface coverage of the EIS or PDP test (Tables 3 and 4), K ads and C inh denote the adsorption equilibrium constant and corrosion inhibitor content, respectively [36]. As demonstrated in Fig. 11 , the linear correlation coefficient (R2) values of CDs are 0.9180 (EIS) and 0.9444 (PDP), and the R2 values of CDAs are 0.9959 (EIS) and 0.9875 (PDP). This testifies that CDAs and CDs conform to Langmuir isothermal adsorption model. The Gibbs free energy (ΔG0 ads) value is calculated from the equation: ΔG0 ads = -RTIn (1000 Kads), where T presents the thermodynamic temperature (298 K) and R signifies the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1) [27]. Normally, the ΔG0 ads value larger than −20 kJ/mol is physical absorption, and smaller than −40 kJ/mol is chemical absorption [37]. The ΔG0 ads values of CDs are −22.51 kJ/mol (EIS) and −22.64 kJ/mol (PDP), and the ΔG0 ads values of CDAs are −25.91 kJ/mol (EIS) and −25.46 kJ/mol (PDP). This verifies both CDs and CDAs adsorb on the carbon steel surface via physisorption and chemisorption. The standard adsorption enthalpy (ΔH°) is ascertained by Van't Hoff equation:
ln
(
K
a d s
)
= −
△
H 0
R T
+ constant
, and entropy (ΔS°) is confirmed by the formula:
△
S 0
=
△
H 0
− △
G 0
T
[38,39]. Specifically, the ΔS° values of CDs are 93.62 J mol−1 K−1 (EIS) and 94.50 J mol−1 K−1 (PDP), and the ΔS° values of CDAs are 116.48 J mol−1 K−1 (EIS) and 113.42 J mol−1 K−1 (PDP). In general, a positive value of ΔS° promotes the adsorption of corrosion inhibitor on the metal surface [40], in agreement with the ΔG0 ads results.

The surface states of carbon steel in diverse conditions are analyzed utilizing a 3D scanner, SEM, EDS, and XPS. After 96-h exposure to 1 mol/L HCl solution (Fig. 12 a), the sample undergoes significant corrosion and the surface roughness (S a) is 23.905 μm. After incorporating 200 mg/L CDs (Fig. 12b), the S a value merely reduces to 13.285 μm. By contrast, 200 mg/L CDAs decrease the S a value to 1.276 μm (Fig. 12c). The above results signify that CDAs possess a greater protective effect on carbon steel than CDs, better restraining the corrosion and making the surface smoother. The corrosive products are generated on the surface after 96-h exposure to 1 mol/L HCl solution (Fig. 13 a), verifying serious corrosion. The incorporation of 200 mg/L CDs only slightly inhibits the corrosion of carbon steel, and some corrosion products are still observed (Fig. 13b). Notably, the carbon steel surface becomes flatter after incorporating 200 mg/L CDAs, and nearly no corrosion products are detected (Fig. 13c). This displays that CDAs significantly mitigate the corrosion by comparison to CDs, in agreement with the 3D scanner results. The original composition of carbon steel is mainly Fe and C, but small amounts of O and Cl are detected on account of oxidation [12] and immersion in 1 mol/L HCl solution (Fig. 13d–f and Table 5 ). In contrast to the blank group, the element content of carbon steel surface changes after adding 200 mg/L CDs/CDAs, especially the variation of the CDAs-containing group is more obvious. To be specific, the Fe and C contents of the 200 mg/L CDAs-containing group are 83.96% and 5.36%, respectively, higher than those of the blank group (Fe 77.28%, C 4.69%) and the CDs-containing group (Fe 76.88%, C 5.22%). And the O content of the 200 mg/L CDAs-containing group is 10.05%, smaller than that of the blank group (17.02%) and the CDs-containing group (16.74%). This denotes that CDAs own a better capacity to retard the corrosion and reduce the generation of corrosion products (e.g., α-FeOOH, γ-FeOOH, α-Fe2O3, and Fe3O4) [41], consistent with the consequences of 3D scanner and SEM.

To sum up, the presence of CDAs results in fewer corrosion products and a flatter carbon steel surface than CDs. This is due to the stronger adsorption ability of CDAs on the carbon steel surface, thereby protecting the carbon steel and inhibiting its corrosion more effectively.

Quantum chemical calculations and MD simulations are adopted to provide insight into the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDAs. The molecular models of CDs and CDAs are developed based on FTIR and XPS characterizations. The highest occupied molecular orbit (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbit (LUMO) are distributed throughout the CDs/CDAs molecules (Fig. 15 ), conducing to their adsorption on metal surfaces. Based on the frontier molecular orbital theory, E LUMO and E HOMO manifest the abilities of the corrosion inhibitor molecule to obtain and donate electrons, respectively. To be more specific, a smaller value of E LUMO represents a better electron-gaining capacity, while a larger value of E HOMO signifies a greater electron-losing ability [49]. Generally, the smaller the value of the band gap (ΔE, ΔE = E LUMO–E HOMO), the greater the bonding of the corrosion inhibitor molecule to the metal surface [50]. As displayed in Table 7 , the ΔE value of CDAs is 0.262 eV, smaller than that of CDs (1.125 eV), possibly corresponding to its better bonding to the metal surface. CDs and CDAs adsorb in parallel on the Fe (110) surface and completely cover the matrix (Fig. 16 ). The interaction energy (E interaction) between the metal surface and the corrosion inhibitor molecule is calculated adopting the following equation:
E interaction
=
E total
−
(
E
surface + solution
+
E molecule
)
, where E total expresses the total energy of the system, E surface + solution represents the energy of the surface and the solution, and E molecule means the energy of the molecule [51]. And the binding energy (E binding) is obtained by the formula:
E binding
= −
E interaction
. In general, the larger the value of E binding, the greater the interaction between the corrosion inhibitor molecule and the metal, and the stronger the adsorption capacity of the corrosion inhibitor molecule on the metal surface [28,52]. Specifically, the E binding value of CDAs is 381.25 kJ mol−1, much larger than that of CDs (222.08 kJ mol−1). This manifests that CDAs own greater interaction with the metal, probably contributing to better adsorption on the metal, thereby achieving their corrosion inhibition potential. The above findings are in agreement with quantum chemical calculations.

On account of the aforementioned research, the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDAs for carbon steel in 1 mol/L HCl solution is reasonably revealed (Fig. 17 ). The anodic reaction (Fe - 2e− = Fe2+) and the cathodic reaction (2H+ + 2e− = H2↑) of carbon steel occur in 1 mol/L HCl solution (Fig. 17a). The reactions cause the rapid dissolution of carbon steel and continuous release of H2, thus resulting in severe corrosion. Moreover, Cl− further accelerates the corrosion process. The corrosion is slightly retarded by adding CDs (Fig. 17b), while the incorporation of CDAs immensely retards the corrosion (Fig. 17c). Both CDs and CDAs are enriched with plentiful N- and O-containing groups (illustrated by FTIR and XPS), resulting in splendid long-term dispersion stability in the acidic solution (revealed by UV–vis and PL spectra). Nevertheless, the stronger bonding ability of CDAs to the metal surface makes them better adsorb on the metal surface via physisorption and chemisorption (verified by adsorption isotherm analysis and theoretical calculations), thus CDAs possess a greater capacity to inhibit carbon steel corrosion by notably mitigating its anodic reaction (demonstrated by electrochemical measurements). Eventually, the corrosion products on the carbon steel surface are decreased and the surface becomes flatter (indicated by 3D scanner, SEM, and EDS). To sum up, the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDAs for carbon steel in 1 mol/L HCl solution is that CDAs physically and chemically adsorb on the carbon steel surface more effectively, thereby mitigating corrosion mainly by retarding the anodic reaction of carbon steel.

In this research, the corrosion inhibition ability of CDAs for Q235 carbon steel in 1 mol/L HCl solution is uncovered for the first time, and the related corrosion inhibition mechanism is raised. The primary conclusions are as follows.
(1)
Green and cost-effective CDAs are synthesized by solvothermal treatment of CDs. Furthermore, the affluent N- and O-containing surface groups of CDAs endow them with wonderful long-term dispersion stability in the acid solution, creating favorable conditions for CDAs as corrosion inhibitors in acidic environments.
(2)
The weight loss test and electrochemical measurements display that CDAs possess splendid long-term corrosion inhibition performance. Concretely, the corrosion inhibition effect of 200 mg/L CDAs for carbon steel reaches 90.38% in 1 mol/L HCl solution.
(3)
The corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDAs is reasonably proposed by combining the analyses of adsorption isotherm, corrosion morphologies, and theoretical calculations. That is, CDAs cover the carbon steel surface via physisorption and chemisorption and primarily inhibit the anodic reaction of carbon steel, thereby restraining corrosion.

This research firstly demonstrates the potential of CDAs as corrosion inhibitors under acidic conditions, offering a reference for the research of aggregate-based corrosion inhibitors. In the future, the corrosion inhibition behavior of CDAs in neutral or alkaline environments can be considered, contributing to broadening the application fields of CDAs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Youth Science Fund (No. 52208273), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LQ21E080003), and “Pioneer” and “Leading Goose” R&D Program of Zhejiang, China (No. 2022C03051; 2023C04033). Furthermore, the authors thank Xinfang Cui from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for XPS measurement.
